== Table Description ==
A dataset of warming center locations in Chicago, including facility types, operational hours, addresses, contact information, and geographic coordinates to support public cold-weather relief efforts.

== Column Information ==
[FacilityType] site type: Facility types include Library, Parks, Satellite Senior Center, Chicago Community College, and Community Service Center. Contains 6 facility types, including Library, Parks, Satellite Senior Center, Chicago Community College, Community Service Center
[EntityName] site name: Site names include 'Sheridan Park', 'Horner Park', 'Daley, Richard M.', and 'McFetridge Sports Center'. 139 unique entities identified, including "Sheridan Park", "Horner Park", "Daley, Richard M.", "McFetridge Sports Center", "West Lawn", with an average name length of 13.3 characters.
[Schedule] hours of operation: Hours of operation vary, such as 'M and W 10-6; T and Th 12-8; Fri and Sat 9-5; Sun 1-5', 'M-F 10AM-7PM; SAT 9AM-3PM', and 'M-F: 8:30 a.m. to 4:30 p.m.'. Schedule patterns including 41 unique entries such as "M and W 10-6; T and Th 12-8; Fri and Sat 9-5; Sun 1-5", "M and W 10-6; T and Th 12-8; Fri and Sat 9-5; Sun 1-5", "M-F 10AM-7PM; SAT 9AM-3PM", "M-F 10AM-8PM", "M-F: 8:30 a.m. to 4:30 p.m."

Contains 142 unique addresses or address components, including examples such as "642 W. 43rd St.", "163 E. Pearson St.", "11010 S. Kedzie Ave.", "3436 S. King Dr.", "3030 W. Fullerton Ave.". The average length of the address strings is 18.8 characters.
[AdministrativeUnit] city: City is consistently listed as 'Chicago' or 'CHICAGO'. Contains 2 administrative units, including Chicago, CHICAGO, with an average identifier length of 7.0 characters.
[AdministrativeUnit] state: State is uniformly 'IL'. Contains 1 administrative units, including IL, with an average identifier length of 2.0 characters.
[ZipCode] zip: Zip codes include '60617', '60628', '60632', and '60623'; 19% nulls reflect optional reporting. Zip codes ranging from 5 to 5 characters, containing 52 unique values such as "60617", "60628", "60628", "60632", "60623"
[ContactAndResource] phone: Phone numbers include '(312) 747-0511', '312-744-2580', and '(312) 747-0193'; 19% nulls reflect optional contact info. Contains 115 unique contact or resource entries, including "(312) 747-0511", "312-744-2580", "(312) 747-0193", "(312) 744-1998", "(312) 744-0156". The average length of entries is 13.4 characters.
[GeographicCoordinate] location: Geographic coordinates range from -87.83 to -87.53, with a mean of -87.68, representing Chicago-area locations. Geographic coordinates ranging from -87.83 to -87.53 with a mean value of -87.68

== Table Description ==
A comprehensive dataset of COPA (Civilian Office of Police Accountability) cases tracking incidents involving police officers, including complaint details, officer demographics, case status, and temporal attributes. The data spans 2022 to 2024 and includes administrative, demographic, and case outcome information, with many fields intentionally sparse due to case processing stages and privacy constraints.

== Column Information ==
[RecordIdentifier] log_no: Unique case identifier for COPA investigations, e.g., "2022-0004694", "2023-0004080", "2022-0004921" Contains 8,513 unique record identifiers, including examples such as "2022-0004694", "2023-0004080", "2022-0004921", "2023-0005654", "2023-0003704".
[Timestamp] complaint_date: Date and time when the complaint was filed, e.g., "2022-04-14T10:57", "2024-06-14T16:05", "2023-0003704T09:22" Timestamps ranging from 2022-04-14T10:57 to 2024-06-14T16:05 across 10,000 records
[AdministrativeRole] assignment: Administrative unit handling the case, e.g., "BIA", "COPA" Contains 2 administrative roles and positions, including BIA, COPA
[IncidentType] case_type: Type of incident reported, e.g., "Complaint", "Notification", "Miscellaneous" 3 incident types, including Complaint, Notification, Miscellaneous
[StatusAndLicense] current_status: Current stage of case resolution, e.g., "Closed No Allegations - Insufficient Object Evidence", "Pending Superintendent Review", "Administratively Closed" Contains 36 unique status or license categories, including Closed No Allegations - Insufficient Object Evidence, Pending Superintendent Review, Non-Disciplinary Closure, Pending Civil Suit, Administratively Closed. Average label length is 29.1 characters.
[EducationalClassification] current_category: Internal categorization of case progress, e.g., "Closed-No Allegation", "In Process-Allegation Pending" Educational classifications including 2 unique labels such as Closed-No Allegation, In Process-Allegation Pending. Common categories include "Closed-No Allegation", "In Process-Allegation Pending", "In Process-Allegation Pending", "In Process-Allegation Pending", "In Process-Allegation Pending".
[IncidentType] finding_code: Final determination of the investigation, e.g., "No Finding" 1 incident types, including No Finding
[IndicatorFlag] police_shooting: Binary flag indicating whether a police shooting occurred, e.g., 0 (no), 1 (yes) — 76 total shootings recorded Binary indicator flags with a sum of 76 occurrences across 2,995 records (mode: 0)
[AdministrativeZone] beat: Police beat or patrol zone where incident occurred, e.g., "1134", "1133", "0231" 277 administrative zones (e.g., "1134", "Unknown", "1133", "0231", "1922") with an average identifier length of 4.3 characters
[DemographicCategory] race_of_involved_officer: Race of the involved officer, e.g., "White", "Black or African American", "Hispanic, Latino, or Spanish Origin" 6 demographic categories, including White, Hispanic, Latino, or Spanish Origin, Black or African American, Unknown, Asian or Pacific Islander
[DemographicCategory] sex_of_involved_officer: Sex of the involved officer, e.g., "Male", "Female", "Unknown" 3 demographic categories, including Male, Female, Unknown
[DemographicCategory] age_of_involved_officer: Age range of the involved officer, e.g., "30-39", "40-49", "Unknown" 8 demographic categories, including 30-39, 20-29, 40-49, Unknown, 50-59
[DemographicCategory] years_on_force_of_involved_officer: Years of service of the involved officer, e.g., "5-9", "20-24", "Unknown" 8 demographic categories, including 5-9, 0-4, Unknown, 20-24, 15-19
[TemporalInterval] complaint_hour: Hour of day when complaint was made, e.g., 00:00, 14:00, 23:00 Temporal intervals spanning from 2024-01-01T00:00 to 2024-01-01T23:00 across 10,000 records
[TemporalInterval] complaint_day: Day of week or specific day of complaint, e.g., 01:00, 04:00, 07:00 Temporal intervals spanning from 2024-01-01T01:00 to 2024-01-01T07:00 across 10,000 records
[TemporalInterval] complaint_month: Month of complaint filing, e.g., 01:00, 06:00, 12:00 Temporal intervals spanning from 2024-01-01T01:00 to 2024-01-01T12:00 across 10,000 records

== Table Description ==
A comprehensive dataset of parking permit zones with administrative, geographic, and status information for 10,000 records, including zone boundaries, street address ranges, ward assignments, and permit status.

== Table Description ==
A comprehensive dataset of utility hit tickets issued in Chicago, capturing details of utility excavation incidents including report metadata, geographic locations, involved entities, and facility information. The data spans from 2015 to 2023 and includes records of hits on gas, electric, water, and communication lines, with associated reporting and ownership details.

== Column Information ==
[RecordIdentifier] hit report no: Unique identifier for each hit report, e.g., '601416207', '600120209', '601319430'. Contains 2,546 unique record identifiers, including examples such as "601416207", "600120209", "601319430", "601216913", "601280561".
[Timestamp] hit date: Timestamp of when the hit was reported, e.g., '2015-09-11T12:00', '2023-12-15T12:00'. Timestamps ranging from 2015-09-11T12:00 to 2023-12-15T12:00 across 10,000 records
[StatusAndLicense] hit report status: Status of the hit report, e.g., 'Complete', 'AutoAppr', 'Approved', 'Duplicate', 'Canceled'. Contains 5 unique status or license categories, including Complete, AutoAppr, Approved, Duplicate, Canceled. Average label length is 8.1 characters.
[IncidentType] notice type: Type of notice issued, e.g., 'Initial ticket', 'Update ticket', 'Cancellation notice'. 3 incident types, including Initial ticket, Update ticket, Cancellation notice

== Table Description ==
This table contains a comprehensive, deprecated directory of mental health and community health service providers in Chicago, as maintained by the Chicago Department of Public Health (CDPH) until November 2023. It includes 171 organizations with 46 attributes spanning core operational details, service offerings, and accessibility metrics. Core attributes (columns 0–24) identify organizations by name and location, capture physical addresses, zip codes, contact information, operating hours (including evenings and weekends), insurance acceptance policies, sliding scale fee availability, and service targeting by age group and geographic area (community areas, wards, police districts). Supplemental attributes (columns 25–48) detail specialized services such as crisis intervention, substance use treatment, behavioral health programs, telehealth availability, language access (e.g., interpreters, multilingual staff), and support for special populations (e.g., LGBTQ+, veterans, youth, homeless). Many fields are binary flags indicating service availability, while others contain free-text descriptions, with high null rates reflecting voluntary or inconsistent reporting rather than data absence. The dataset was designed to support equity-focused mapping and resource allocation by enabling analysis of mental health service distribution across Chicago neighborhoods, with emphasis on accessibility for uninsured and underserved populations.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive school-level performance and demographic data for all Chicago Public Schools (CPS) in School Year 2018–2019 (SY1819), spanning 654 schools and 182 columns. It integrates foundational metadata (school name, type, address, contact info, fiscal year), academic achievement metrics (reading and math proficiency and growth via NWEA MAP assessments for grades 2–8), survey-based climate indicators (safety, leadership, family involvement, staff collaboration), attendance and discipline data (suspensions, misconducts, student/staff attendance), student outcomes (on-track rates, graduation, college enrollment and persistence), standardized test performance (PSAT/SAT scores for grades 9–11), geographic coordinates, award recognitions, and comparative benchmarks against district-wide CPS averages. The data combines administrative records, survey responses, and state report card information, with many metrics presented as both percentage values and categorical labels. High null rates in certain columns (e.g., NWEA, PSAT/SAT) reflect sparse reporting for specific grade cohorts rather than data quality issues. This table serves as a holistic progress report for evaluating school performance across academic, behavioral, environmental, and longitudinal outcome dimensions.

== Table Description ==
A log of FOIA requests submitted to the Chicago Police Board, capturing requestor details, organizational affiliations, request descriptions, and associated deadlines between 2010 and 2019.

== Column Information ==
[PersonalIdentity] requestor name: Requestor names such as Tim Novak & Chris Fusco, Christina Berger, and Sam Stecklow; includes individuals and paired requesters. Contains 260 unique personal identities, including names such as "Tim Novak & Chris Fusco", "Christina Berger", "Sam Stecklow", "Tim Novak", "Sheila Hubbard". The average name length is 13.4 characters.
[BusinessEntity] organization: Organizations making requests, including Chicago Tribune, Chicago Sun-Times, and Chicago Justice Project; some entries are unassigned. 117 business entities identified, including None identified, Chicago Tribune, Chicago Sun-Times, Chicago Justice Project, City Bureau. Average name length is 17.9 characters.
[DescriptionText] description of request: Descriptions of records sought, such as 'Records pertaining to Police Board disciplinary cases' and 'Disciplinary records pertaining to a Chicago police officer.' Text descriptions with an average length of 58.6 characters. Key terms include: police, records, pertaining, disciplinary, board. Example entries: "Records pertaining to Police Board disciplinary cases", "Disciplinary records pertaining to a Chicago police officer.", "Records pertaining to a Police Board disciplinary case", "Records pertaining to Police Board disciplinary cases", "Disciplinary records pertaining to a Chicago police officer.".
[Timestamp] date received: Date the request was received, ranging from 2010-05-05 to 2019-07-18. Timestamps ranging from 2010-05-05T00:00 to 2019-07-18T00:00 across 473 records
[Timestamp] 5-day response deadline: Five-day response deadline calculated from the received date, ranging from 2010-05-12 to 2019-07-25. Timestamps ranging from 2010-05-12T00:00 to 2019-07-25T00:00 across 473 records
[AdministrativeUnit] Organization: Administrative unit handling the request — exclusively 'Chicago Police Board' — though all values are null, indicating this is a metadata field inferred from context. Contains 1 administrative units, including Chicago Police Board, with an average identifier length of 20.0 characters.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive linguistic demographic data for Chicago's 78 community areas during the 2008–2012 period, derived from U.S. Census surveys. It includes for each community area: a unique identifier, the name of the community, the predominant non-English language spoken at home (by percentage), and detailed speaker counts for 21 specific languages or language groups. Additionally, it provides aggregated language speaker counts across the entire city for 22 broader language categories—including high-volume languages such as Spanish, Polish, and Serbo-Croatian, and low-volume categories like Other Native North American and Scandinavian—along with metadata fields indicating the fiscal year, geographic region, and data category. The dataset captures the full spectrum of linguistic diversity in Chicago, enabling analysis of neighborhood-level language distribution and citywide language trends over the five-year period.

== Column Information ==
[AdministrativeUnit] community area: Unique numeric identifier for each of Chicago's 78 community areas, such as 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5. Contains 78 administrative units, including 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, with an average identifier length of 1.9 characters.
[EntityName] community area name: Name of the community area, including examples like 'Portage Park', 'Hyde Park', and 'O'Hare'. 78 unique entities identified, including "Portage Park", "Hyde Park", "Fuller Park", "O'Hare", "Avondale", with an average name length of 11.1 characters.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive records of homicides and non-fatal shootings, designed to support data-driven violence reduction strategies. It includes 10,000 rows with 38 columns that capture foundational incident details such as unique case identifiers, timestamps, geographic locations (block, zip code, ward, community area, beat), and demographic profiles of victims (age, sex, race). It further enriches these records with detailed victimization characteristics, including full victim names, FBI and IUCR classification codes, precise temporal and geographic coordinates, administrative district mappings, and involvement of outreach organizations. Together, these attributes enable multi-dimensional spatial-temporal analysis, demographic profiling, and targeted intervention planning for urban violence prevention efforts.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive public health statistics on low birth weight rates across Chicago community areas from 1999 to 2009. It includes annual data on total births, observed percentages of low birth weight births, and corresponding 95% confidence interval bounds (lower and upper) for each year. The data is structured to enable spatial (by community area) and temporal (year-over-year) analysis of maternal and infant health outcomes. Columns 0–24 cover the period 1999–2004, while columns 25–49 cover 2004–2009, with additional metadata columns providing context such as year, geographic location, and data reliability warnings for years with sparse birth counts. The table supports epidemiological research, public health policy evaluation, and equity-focused interventions targeting high-risk neighborhoods.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive identifying, geographic, administrative, and educational metadata for all Chicago Public Schools (CPS) during the 2013–2014 academic year. It includes core school identifiers (e.g., system IDs), official and common school names, physical addresses with ZIP codes, city and state affiliations, and categorical classifications for school type (e.g., elementary, high school) and level. Additionally, it captures administrative and spatial governance data such as school districts, community areas, political wards, and congressional districts, along with grade-level structures (e.g., PK–8, 9–12) and precise geographic coordinates (latitude/longitude). The dataset enables detailed spatial analysis, enrollment zone mapping, and administrative oversight across Chicago’s neighborhoods and political boundaries, serving as a foundational resource for educational planning, policy analysis, and geographic information systems (GIS) applications.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata for youth and community programs under the 'My CHI. My Future.' initiative in Chicago, encompassing 10,000 programs across 40 attributes. It captures core program identity (name, description, type), organizational context (provider, partner affiliations), demographic targeting (age groups, underserved populations), geographic location (neighborhoods, zip codes, coordinates), scheduling details (days, times, duration), and digital access points (websites, registration links). Operational attributes include contact information, pricing structures, scholarship availability, transportation and meal services, accessibility features, program visibility flags (e.g., featured status), and custom categorical tags for equity-focused segmentation. Many fields exhibit intentional nulls, reflecting sparsity in optional or context-dependent data rather than data quality issues. The table enables program discovery, eligibility filtering, equitable outreach, and administrative management by linking participants to educational, recreational, and skill-building opportunities offered by public and nonprofit providers across Chicago.

== Table Description == This table contains a comprehensive profile of all Chicago Public Schools for the 2023–2024 school year (SY2324), encompassing institutional metadata, demographic and enrollment data, operational characteristics, academic performance metrics, and administrative leadership details. It includes school identifiers, administrative units, school type and level classifications, physical and digital contact information (addresses, websites, social media), grade levels offered, and dress code policies. Student demographics are detailed by total enrollment, income eligibility, special education status, English learner status, and racial/ethnic breakdowns. Operational data includes school hours, after-school program availability, transportation options, and specialized support services (e.g., bilingual, refugee, special education). Academic performance is captured through college enrollment rates, graduation rates, and narrative ratings or classifications. == Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive school-level performance and contextual metadata for all Chicago Public Schools during the 2015–2016 school year (SY1516). It combines 153 columns spanning foundational administrative details (school name, address, phone, type, location), demographic data, academic performance metrics from NWEA MAP assessments (reading and math for grades 2–8), survey-based climate indicators (family involvement, student/staff perceptions), certification statuses (arts, health), attendance and discipline statistics (suspensions, dropouts), academic growth and attainment rates (categorized into qualitative tiers such as 'Very Strong' or 'Weak'), college readiness indicators (on-track rates, college enrollment/persistence), student mobility metrics, and financial and award-related annotations. Each metric is presented in both quantitative (percentages, ratios) and categorical (performance labels) formats, with many fields aggregated across two years (2014–15 and 2015–16) for trend analysis. Null values indicate missing or inapplicable data for specific schools or assessments. The dataset serves as a holistic evaluation tool for school accountability, resource allocation, and improvement planning within the Chicago Public Schools system.

Laboratory surveillance data for influenza, COVID-19, RSV, and other respiratory viruses, tracking weekly testing volumes, positivity rates, and cumulative trends across multiple seasons from 2010 to 2024.

[Timestamp] week_end: End date of the surveillance week, spanning from 2010-10-09 to 2024-06-08 (e.g., 2020-10-10, 2023-01-07, 2024-06-02). Timestamps ranging from 2010-10-09T00:00 to 2024-06-08T00:00 across 2,420 records

[PerformanceRatio] lab_pct_positive: Percentage of specimens testing positive in the week, ranging from 0 to 1 (e.g., 0.02, 0.15, 0.42). Performance ratios ranging from 0 to 1 with an average of 0.05 across 2,393 records.
[VolumeCount] lab_tot_tested_cumulative: Cumulative total specimens tested since the start of the season, ranging from 0 to 471,126 (e.g., 12,345, 250,000, 400,123). Aggregated volume counts ranging from 0 to 471,126 (total: 95,230,636, average: 39351.50)
[VolumeCount] lab_tot_positive_cumulative: Cumulative total positive specimens since the start of the season, ranging from 0 to 54,521 (e.g., 1,200, 15,678, 45,201). Aggregated volume counts ranging from 0 to 54,521 (total: 7,167,777, average: 2961.89)
[PerformanceRatio] lab_pct_positive_cumulative: Cumulative percentage of positive specimens since the start of the season, ranging from 0 to 0.33 (e.g., 0.03, 0.08, 0.22). Performance ratios ranging from 0 to 0.33 with an average of 0.06 across 2,403 records.
[Timestamp] current_week_ending: End date of the current reporting week in the season, ranging from 2023-10-07 to 2024-09-28 (e.g., 2023-10-14, 2024-01-06, 2024-09-21). Timestamps ranging from 2023-10-07T00:00 to 2024-09-28T00:00 across 2,420 records

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata and performance metrics for all Chicago Public Schools during the 2022–2023 school year (SY2223), encompassing administrative, academic, climate, and contextual dimensions. It includes foundational school information such as ID, name, address, contact details, type, and category, alongside survey response rates, wellness and arts certifications, and NWEA assessment data for grades 2–8 in reading and math, presented as paired percentage and qualitative label columns. Additional metrics cover attendance, suspensions, discipline trends, school climate perceptions, and year-over-year comparisons from 2021–2022. Academic outcomes include on-track rates, graduation rates, college enrollment and persistence, chronic truancy, and student mobility, each with CPS-wide benchmark comparisons. Supplemental data includes PSAT/SAT performance for grades 9–11, geographic coordinates, school recognition status, and narrative support system descriptions. While many fields—particularly growth metrics and some survey submetrics—are null, this likely reflects data unavailability, pending calculation, or placeholder status rather than absence of intent. The table serves as a holistic performance dashboard for CPS school evaluation, integrating operational, academic, and environmental indicators for accountability and improvement planning.

== Table Description ==
This table contains a comprehensive profile of all 655 Chicago Public Schools for the School Year 2021–2022 (SY2122), integrating administrative, demographic, operational, academic, and geographic metadata. It includes foundational identifiers (school ID, name, type), leadership and contact details (principal, email, phone, social media), physical addresses with precise geographic coordinates and spatial geometry, and network affiliations. The table captures enrollment data segmented by demographic and program status (e.g., ELL, special education, free/reduced lunch), grade levels offered, preschool/kindergarten schedules, and dress code policies. Operational details include school hours, transportation modes, Title I eligibility, bilingual and special education services, and student support flags. Academic performance metrics such as ACT scores, college enrollment rates, and graduation rates are included alongside overall school ratings. Additionally, contact information for up to seven staff members is recorded, along with enrollment status and date ranges for GoCPS programs. The dataset enables holistic analysis of school characteristics, equity indicators, resource allocation, and performance outcomes across the Chicago Public Schools district.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive public health screening data on elevated blood lead levels in children aged 0–6 years across all 79 community areas of Chicago, spanning the years 1999 to 2013. For each year, the data includes three key metrics: (1) the total number of children screened (VolumeCount), (2) the screening rate per 100 children (with 95% confidence intervals), and (3) the percentage of screened children with elevated blood lead levels (PerformanceRatio), also with 95% confidence intervals. The 122 columns are organized chronologically across four time batches (1999–2002, 2002–2006, 2006–2010, and 2010–2013), with each year represented by three corresponding columns for counts, rates, and elevated level percentages with statistical bounds. The final three columns (Year, Location, AgeGroup) serve as metadata, though they are largely redundant as they remain constant (Year: annual values; Location: Chicago; AgeGroup: 0–6 years) across all rows. The dataset captures longitudinal trends in lead exposure surveillance, showing increasing screening coverage and declining prevalence of elevated blood lead levels over the 15-year period, making it a critical resource for public health monitoring and intervention evaluation.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata for 10,000 traffic crash incidents, combining foundational event details with spatial-temporal and injury attributes. The first 24 columns capture crash identification, timing, road and environmental conditions, vehicle behavior, contributing causes, and reporting information, providing a baseline for analyzing incident patterns and response dynamics. The remaining 24 columns extend this with precise geographic data (coordinates, geometry), street location (name and direction), administrative beat, injury severity and counts, work zone indicators, and temporal context (hour, day, month), enabling detailed spatial-temporal analysis of crash hotspots, injury trends, and environmental influences. Together, these columns support holistic investigation of traffic safety, infrastructure impact, and emergency response effectiveness.

== Column Information ==

[Timestamp] crash_date: Exact timestamp of the crash event, e.g., '2024-05-20T20:00', '2024-06-15T14:18', '2024-06-20T03:00' Timestamps ranging from 2024-05-20T20:00 to 2024-06-20T03:00 across 10,000 records
[PerformanceMetric] posted_speed_limit: Maximum posted speed limit at crash location, e.g., 25, 35, 55 Performance metrics ranging from 0 to 55 with an average of 28.56 across 10,000 records.

[Categorical] device_condition: Condition of the traffic control device at time of crash, e.g., 'FUNCTIONING PROPERLY', 'FUNCTIONING IMPROPERLY', 'UNKNOWN' Contains 7 unique categories, including NO CONTROLS, FUNCTIONING PROPERLY, UNKNOWN, OTHER, FUNCTIONING IMPROPERLY

== Table Description ==
This table contains daily rolling average rates for COVID-19 cases, deaths, and hospitalizations from March 2020 to May 2024, aggregated at the national level and stratified by age group, sex, and race/ethnicity. All rates are expressed as performance ratios (e.g., per 100,000 population per day), except for a subset of total case columns which are absolute counts. The dataset captures temporal trends and demographic disparities in COVID-19 outcomes, consistently showing higher rates among older age groups and Black non-Latinx populations. The 97 columns are organized into four logical batches: (1) base metrics and initial demographic breakdowns for cases, deaths, and hospitalizations; (2) additional stratifications for deaths and hospitalizations with total case counts; (3) continuous numerical measures of all three outcomes across demographic subgroups; and (4) final demographic-specific death and hospitalization rates, including a placeholder 'Year' column with null values indicating historical context rather than fiscal year alignment. The table enables longitudinal analysis of pandemic burden across intersecting demographic dimensions, supporting public health equity research and policy evaluation.

== Table Description ==
A log of Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests submitted to the Public Building Commission, capturing request metadata such as receipt date, submission method, requester details, project context, requested information, due dates, and administrative unit.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata for 259 public service locations across Chicago, including public libraries, senior centers, community technology centers, and city-affiliated service points. It captures foundational organizational identity, physical and digital contact details, operational hours, facility amenities, and volunteer engagement structures. The data further extends to detailed operational attributes such as digital access (internet, WiFi, PC use policies), assistive technology availability, training programs, transportation options, parking availability, and staff contact information. While many fields related to technical specifications (e.g., upload/download speeds) and volunteer details exhibit high null rates, this reflects optional or inconsistently reported operational data rather than data quality issues. The table is designed to support service coordination, geographic mapping, accessibility planning, and outreach initiatives for public service providers and city administrators.

== Table Description ==

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive 2012–2013 Chicago Public High School Progress Report Card data, encompassing 92 schools and 61 metadata and performance dimensions. It includes foundational school identifiers (name, address, network), demographic and geographic information, academic performance metrics (overall ratings, probation status, graduation and dropout rates, standardized test scores), college readiness indicators (college eligibility and enrollment), attendance and retention rates, school climate factors (leadership, teaching quality, safety), operational data (suspension rates), binary indicators for certifications and awards, geographic coordinates (latitude/longitude), and contextual metadata (year, organizational classification). The dataset enables holistic analysis of school accountability, equity, performance trends, and operational health across Chicago’s high school system during the 2012–2013 academic year, with many metrics tracking changes from the prior year (2011–2012).

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata for approximately 10,000 buildings in a metropolitan area (likely Chicago), capturing foundational spatial, administrative, and structural attributes alongside detailed provenance and derived characteristics. The 44 columns are divided into two logical groups: the first 22 columns include core building identifiers (e.g., unique IDs), geographic geometry, street addresses, building names, construction and modification timestamps, and basic lifecycle metadata; the second 22 columns extend this with administrative tracking data (edit/QC timestamps, user IDs, data sources), precise geographic coordinates (latitude/longitude), unit counts, number of stories, square footage, building condition ratings, and geometric measurements. Most fields exhibit high null rates, reflecting incomplete legacy records and optional data entry practices common in municipal or spatial data systems. The table serves as a central reference for building inventory management, urban planning, geospatial analysis, and data quality auditing.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata for 311 service requests in Chicago, encompassing 10,000 records across 39 columns. It captures the full lifecycle of service requests—from creation to closure—with core identifiers, incident types, timestamps, and administrative assignments. Geographic details include street addresses, zip codes, city, state, latitude/longitude coordinates, and spatial geometry. Temporal attributes such as hour, day of week, and month of creation enable time-based analysis. Administrative hierarchies are represented through ward, police district/beat, community area, sanitation district, and electrical utility zones, facilitating jurisdictional mapping and resource allocation. The table also includes flags for duplicate or legacy records, ensuring data integrity for analytical and operational use. Designed for spatial, temporal, and administrative analysis, this dataset supports city-wide service response optimization, performance monitoring, and public service planning.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata and performance metrics for all Chicago Public Schools (CPS) during the 2021–2022 School Year (SY2122), structured across 182 columns. It integrates foundational school information (ID, name, location, contact details, award history), survey response rates (student, teacher, parent), certification statuses (e.g., Healthy and Creative Schools), and a wide array of academic and climate indicators. Academic data includes NWEA growth and performance metrics for grades 2–8 in Math and Reading, though many fields are null due to reporting thresholds or incomplete data collection. Performance indicators span discipline and suspension rates, attendance, dropout rates, chronic truancy, student mobility, on-track status, graduation rates, and college enrollment/persistence — all compared against CPS-wide benchmarks across FY2020–21 and FY2021–22. The table also includes SAT/PSAT scores (Grade 11), geographic coordinates, narrative descriptions of social-emotional learning supports, and survey-based ratings on school climate (safety, instruction, leadership, community engagement). While the structure is designed for longitudinal and comparative analysis, significant null values reflect data suppression, low student counts, or incomplete reporting, particularly in NWEA and SAT metrics. This table serves as the primary holistic record for evaluating school performance, equity, and climate in CPS for SY2122.

== Table Description ==
This table contains administrative, geographic, and educational metadata for 48 high schools in Chicago during the 2017-2018 school year, including school identifiers, names, addresses, spatial boundaries, grade levels, school types, and geometric measurements.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive public opinion and budget allocation data from the 2020 Budget Survey, capturing respondent preferences and funding assignments across 55 dimensions. It includes binary flags indicating support for specific revenue sources (e.g., taxes, fees) and expense areas (e.g., public services), along with categorical preferences (Increase/Keep/Reduce) for budget adjustments in core city departments such as police, infrastructure, and community services. Additionally, it records respondent-assigned funding allocations (in $1,000 units) for key departments, as well as categorical responses on proposed changes to taxes and spending levels. The table concludes with two metadata fields: a record counter and the fiscal year (2020), enabling tracking and temporal context. With 7,347 rows, this dataset provides a detailed snapshot of citizen priorities and financial expectations for municipal budgeting.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata for business licenses issued in Chicago and surrounding areas, spanning 10,000 records across 34 columns. It combines core administrative and geographic identifiers (such as unique license IDs, legal and DBA business names, physical addresses, city, state, ZIP, ward, precinct, police district, license code, business activity, and administrative account/site numbers) with the full lifecycle and spatial attributes of each license. Lifecycle data includes application dates, approval dates, issuance dates, expiration dates, status changes, payment records, and renewal history, enabling tracking of compliance and regulatory timelines. Spatial coordinates and jurisdictional zones support location-based regulation and enforcement. Together, these fields form a complete operational and geographic profile of licensed businesses, suitable for regulatory oversight, urban planning, compliance auditing, and business intelligence applications.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive historical daily COVID-19 testing data aggregated by person, spanning from March 2020 to May 2021. It includes 434 rows (one per day) and 59 columns that capture total testing volumes, confirmed positive cases, and detailed demographic stratifications. The data is broken down by age groups, gender, and race/ethnicity, distinguishing between individuals who tested positive and those who tested negative. Additionally, it includes categories for known and unknown demographic attributes, enabling analysis of testing disparities and trends across population subgroups. Columns cover daily metrics such as date, day of week, cumulative and daily counts, and demographic-specific testing and positivity rates, providing a granular view of testing patterns and equity during the early pandemic period.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive historical daily COVID-19 testing data in the United States from March 2020 to May 2024, spanning 1540 rows and 59 columns. It captures aggregated test volumes, including total tests conducted, positive tests, and negative/inconclusive results, stratified by key demographic dimensions: age group, gender, and race/ethnicity. Each row represents a single day, with temporal context provided by date and day of week. The data is structured to enable longitudinal trend analysis and equity-focused monitoring of testing patterns across U.S. population subgroups. Columns are divided into two logical batches: the first (0–29) includes overall test metrics and demographic breakdowns for total and positive tests, while the second (30–59) provides detailed daily counts of positive and negative test results by the same demographic categories, along with a Year metadata column for temporal aggregation. This dataset supports public health surveillance, resource allocation decisions, and disparities research in pandemic response.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive preterm birth statistics for all 78 Chicago community areas across the years 1999 to 2009. For each year (1999–2004 and 2004–2009), the data includes three key metrics per community area: the raw count of preterm births, the percentage of preterm births relative to total births, and a lower confidence interval bound (95%) for the percentage rate. Starting in 2004, the dataset expands to include an upper confidence interval bound as well, providing full 95% CI ranges for later years. Additional metadata columns capture data reliability warnings, geographic region identifiers, year, and category labels, though these are largely sparse or redundant due to their fixed nature across the dataset. The structure enables longitudinal trend analysis of preterm birth rates at the community level, with built-in statistical uncertainty measures to support public health research and policy evaluation.

== Table Description ==
This dataset captures detailed trip records from a 2019 e-scooter pilot program, including temporal, spatial, and administrative metadata for 10,000 trips across Chicago. Each record contains start and end timestamps, trip distance and duration, census tract and community area identifiers, geographic coordinates, and geometry points for trip origins and destinations. The dataset is annotated with fiscal year and category metadata indicating the pilot context, with notable nulls in administrative zones reflecting incomplete geocoding or data collection.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive community-level and city-wide birth statistics for Chicago from 1999 to 2009, spanning 78 community areas across 11 years. Columns 0–24 provide annual birth counts, birth rates per 1,000 population, and 95% confidence intervals (lower and upper bounds) for each community area from 1999 to 2004. Columns 25–48 extend this data to 2004–2009, including additional metadata fields for Region, Year, and Category to contextualize city-wide trends. The dataset enables analysis of temporal fertility patterns, geographic disparities in birth rates, and statistical uncertainty in rate estimates across Chicago’s neighborhoods over a decade.

== Table Description ==
This table contains a comprehensive profile of all Chicago Public Schools for the 2022–2023 school year (SY2223), integrating administrative, demographic, operational, academic, and geographic metadata across 99 columns. It includes foundational identifiers (school ID, name, type), physical and digital contact details, leadership and administrative staff contacts (up to seven roles), and network affiliations. The table captures detailed student enrollment data segmented by race, economic status, special education, and English learner status, alongside grade levels offered and daily schedule information. Operational features include school hours, transportation options, Title I eligibility, language support services, and dress code policies. Academic performance metrics encompass ACT scores, college enrollment rates, graduation rates, and official district ratings with narrative descriptions. Geographic data includes latitude/longitude coordinates and spatial geometry for mapping. Many fields exhibit sparse data due to optional reporting or niche program participation, but collectively they enable holistic school classification, resource allocation analysis, equity assessments, and district-wide planning.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive historical metrics on U.S. hospital capacity during the COVID-19 pandemic, tracking ventilator and ICU bed utilization across both COVID-19 and non-COVID-19 patient populations. It includes detailed breakdowns of total capacity, current usage (by confirmed COVID-19, Persons Under Investigation (PUI), adult, and pediatric categories), available reserves, and surge capacity for ICU and acute non-ICU beds. Additional fields capture daily reporting context via a PDF link and a Year field for temporal analysis. With 1,147 rows and 33 columns, the dataset enables granular analysis of healthcare system strain, resource allocation trends, and capacity planning throughout the pandemic.

== Column Information ==
[Timestamp] date: Timestamps spanning March 2020 to May 2023, capturing daily reporting periods (e.g., 2020-03-19, 2021-12-25, 2023-05-09). Timestamps ranging from 2020-03-19T00:00 to 2023-05-09T00:00 across 1,147 records
[FinancialAmount] ventilators - total capacity: Total ventilator capacity at reporting hospitals, ranging from 960 to 2,231 units (e.g., 1,840 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 960 to 2,231 with an average of 1840.48 across 1,147 entries.
[FinancialAmount] ventilators in use - total: Total ventilators currently in use across all patients, ranging from 247 to 1,117 (e.g., 584 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 247 to 1,117 with an average of 584.28 across 1,147 entries.
[FinancialAmount] ventilators in use - covid-19: Ventilators in use specifically for confirmed COVID-19 patients, ranging from 2 to 361 (e.g., 63 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 2 to 361 with an average of 62.95 across 1,146 entries.
[FinancialAmount] ventilators in use - covid-19 patients: Ventilators in use for patients diagnosed with COVID-19 (similar to column 3, slightly lower values, e.g., 55 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 1 to 338 with an average of 55.24 across 1,146 entries.
[FinancialAmount] ventilators in use - covid-19 pui: Ventilators in use for persons under investigation (PUI) for COVID-19, ranging from 0 to 90 (e.g., 8 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 0 to 90 with an average of 7.70 across 1,146 entries.
[FinancialAmount] ventilators in use - non-covid-19: Ventilators in use for non-COVID-19 patients, ranging from -24 (possible data error) to 995 (e.g., 521 on average). Financial amounts ranging from -24 to 995 with an average of 521.30 across 1,146 entries.
[FinancialAmount] ventilators available - total: Total available ventilators (capacity minus in-use), ranging from 534 to 1,661 (e.g., 1,256 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 534 to 1,661 with an average of 1256.20 across 1,147 entries.
[FinancialAmount] ventilators available - hospital: Available ventilators within the reporting hospital (excluding external caches), ranging from 410 to 1,611 (e.g., 1,205 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 410 to 1,611 with an average of 1205.16 across 1,147 entries.
[FinancialAmount] ventilators available - eamc cache: Available ventilators held in EAMC cache (regional emergency reserve), ranging from 50 to 124 (e.g., 51 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 50 to 124 with an average of 51.03 across 1,147 entries.
[FinancialAmount] ventilator surge capacity: Surge capacity for additional ventilators that can be deployed during emergencies, ranging from 184 to 504 (e.g., 277 on average); sparse data (95% nulls) suggests limited reporting. Financial amounts ranging from 184 to 504 with an average of 277.04 across 54 entries.
[FinancialAmount] icu beds - total capacity: Total ICU bed capacity across facilities, ranging from 922 to 1,515 (e.g., 1,101 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 922 to 1,515 with an average of 1101.33 across 1,147 entries.
[FinancialAmount] icu beds - adult: Adult ICU bed capacity, ranging from 757 to 1,284 (e.g., 928 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 757 to 1,284 with an average of 927.79 across 1,147 entries.
[FinancialAmount] icu beds - pediatric: Pediatric ICU bed capacity, ranging from 164 to 231 (e.g., 174 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 164 to 231 with an average of 173.55 across 1,147 entries.
[FinancialAmount] icu beds in use - total: Total ICU beds currently occupied, ranging from 165 to 1,204 (e.g., 848 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 165 to 1,204 with an average of 847.94 across 1,147 entries.
[FinancialAmount] icu beds in use - covid-19: ICU beds occupied by confirmed COVID-19 patients, ranging from 7 to 524 (e.g., 114 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 7 to 524 with an average of 114.25 across 1,147 entries.
[FinancialAmount] icu beds in use - covid-19 patients: ICU beds occupied by patients diagnosed with COVID-19 (similar to column 15, slightly lower, e.g., 94 on average). Financial amounts ranging from 3 to 443 with an average of 93.71 across 1,147 entries.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive diabetes-related hospitalization statistics for Chicago zip codes spanning the years 2000 to 2011, organized into three temporal batches. It includes raw hospitalization counts, crude (unadjusted) per capita rates, and age-sex adjusted (population-standardized) rates, each accompanied by 95% confidence intervals, enabling robust temporal and geographic trend analysis. The 85 columns are structured as follows: columns 0–29 cover 2000–2004, columns 30–58 cover 2004–2008, and columns 59–87 cover 2008–2011 — each year within these ranges has three corresponding metrics (count, crude rate, adjusted rate) with lower and upper confidence bounds. The table spans 49 rows, each representing a Chicago zip code or geographic subgroup, allowing for neighborhood-level analysis of diabetes burden. Metadata columns (Region, Year, Category) are present but sparse, suggesting they are inherited from a broader data schema and provide minimal additional context. This dataset supports public health research on diabetes disparities, healthcare utilization trends, and the impact of population structure on hospitalization rates over an 11-year period.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive vehicle-level records from traffic crash incidents, capturing detailed information about vehicles involved in crashes, their characteristics, crash circumstances, and associated regulatory/compliance metadata. It includes 71 columns organized into three logical batches: (1) Core vehicle and crash details such as crash identifiers, timestamps, vehicle make/model/year, license state, unit type, travel direction, crash maneuver, towing status, occupant counts, and binary flags for crash conditions (e.g., fire, towed, area impact); (2) Geographic and carrier administrative identifiers, including area-specific impact flags (area_01_i to area_99_i), first point of impact, and carrier reference IDs (CMV ID, USDOT, CCMC, ILCC) — though most carrier fields are nearly entirely null, indicating they are optional or system-derived; and (3) Detailed hazardous material (hazmat) and motor carrier safety (MCS) attributes, such as hazmat/MCS violation flags, out-of-service status, vehicle dimensions (length, width, axles), cargo and body types, and permit classifications — most of which are highly sparse, reflecting incident-specific reporting rather than systematic data collection. The table is designed for crash analysis and safety reporting, with high null rates in carrier and hazmat fields indicating that these are contextual or regulatory add-ons rather than core crash attributes. With 10,000 rows, it serves as a rich but sparsely populated dataset for forensic, regulatory, or statistical analysis of vehicle-involved traffic crashes.

== Table Description ==
This table contains a comprehensive profile of all Chicago Public Schools for School Year 2017–2018 (SY1718), encompassing foundational identifiers, demographic enrollment data, operational characteristics, administrative contacts, geographic information, and program participation. It includes unique school and financial account IDs, school names (short and long), institutional level classifications (elementary, middle, high), contact details (phone, fax, websites, social media), and detailed student demographics by race/ethnicity, low-income status, special education, and English learner status. The table further captures school-specific operational features such as bilingual and Title I program participation, transportation services, school day structure, and participation in initiatives like GO CPS. Administrative contacts are documented across up to seven staff roles, alongside precise geographic coordinates and physical addresses. Network affiliations, enrollment availability dates, and sparse optional service indicators provide additional context for school-level analysis. With 661 rows and 92 columns, this dataset enables detailed profiling for resource allocation, equity analysis, policy planning, and operational management within the Chicago Public Schools system.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive lobbying activity records from the 2012–2016 Lobbyist Registry (deprecated October 2015), capturing foundational and operational details of lobbyists, their employers, clients, and government correspondents. It includes personal and organizational contact information (names, addresses, phone/email/fax), employer and client identifiers with industry classifications, registration metadata (dates, versions, status), and financial indicators such as compensation and expenses. The dataset supports regulatory transparency by documenting lobbying relationships and communications, though many fields exhibit high null rates due to non-mandatory reporting requirements. With 10,000 rows and 56 columns, it serves as a historical record of lobbying disclosures during the 2012–2016 period.

== Table Description ==
A log of Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests submitted to the Streets and Sanitation department, capturing requestor details, request descriptions, timelines, responses, and administrative handling. The dataset includes personal and organizational requestors, vehicle tow and impound-related inquiries, timestamps for request and response cycles, and administrative status indicators, though many response fields are sparse due to their optional or case-specific nature.

== Column Information ==
[PersonalIdentity] requestor name: Names of individuals who submitted FOIA requests, such as Anne McCord, Patricia Bertsch, and Joe Cook. Contains 1,973 unique personal identities, including names such as "Anne McCord", "Patricia Bertsch", "Joe Cook", "Pericles Abbasi", "Yolanda Lippert". The average name length is 14.1 characters.
[BusinessEntity] organization: Organizations or entities making requests, including Asst. State's Attorney, Enterprise Holdings, and Bertsch Properties. 761 business entities identified, including Asst. State's Attorney, Attorney at Law, Enterprise Holdings, First National Assets, Bertsch Properties. Average name length is 22.1 characters.
[DescriptionText] description of request: Detailed descriptions of FOIA requests, often referencing vehicle tow records, case numbers, or impound reports; examples: 'vehicle tow records/case#10CR16581', 'request copies of file record only: Docket #07DS009517', 'vehicle tow, impound, release reports/GMC/case#12CR21700'. Text descriptions with an average length of 104.7 characters. Key terms include: vehicle, tow, case, reports, impound. Example entries: "vehicle tow records/case#10CR16581", "request copies of file record only: Docket #07DS009517", "vehicle tow, impound, release reports/GMC/case#12CR21700", "vehicle tow records/2006 Ford Crown/case#10CR11002", "vehicle tow, impound, release reports/1998 Lincoln Continental/case#13CR1918".
[Timestamp] date received: Date the request was received by the department, ranging from 2005-03-18 to 2018-12-29. Timestamps ranging from 0205-03-18T00:00 to 2018-12-29T00:00 across 9,987 records
[Timestamp] due date: The statutory or internal deadline by which the request was expected to be fulfilled, ranging from 2004-08-20 to 2018-10-16. Timestamps ranging from 0204-08-20T00:00 to 2018-10-16T00:00 across 9,984 records
[Timestamp] date responded to: Date on which the request was formally responded to, spanning from 1947-04-01 to 2018-10-23. Timestamps ranging from 1947-04-01T00:00 to 2018-10-23T00:00 across 9,734 records
[AdministrativeRole] assigned to: Internal staff or role assigned to process the request, such as JG, RC, SMCG; often left blank due to workflow variability. Contains 24 administrative roles and positions, including JG, RC, SMCG, ML, JM
[StatusAndLicense] resp/ docs: Status of document release or requester classification, including redacted, redactions, and Recurrent requester; rarely populated due to case-specific handling. Contains 37 unique status or license categories, including redacted, redactions, Recurrent requester, recurrent requester, yes. Average label length is 14.5 characters.
[TemporalInterval] 5-day ext: Indication of whether a 5-day extension was granted for response; all values are null, suggesting this field is obsolete or unused. Temporal intervals spanning from N/A to N/A across 0 records
[RegulatoryAndAction] clarification/ denial: Reason for denial, clarification provided, or regulatory action taken, such as redacted info, 10/29/14 clarification response, or clarification letter; uniformly null, likely because responses are documented externally. Contains 35 regulatory labels or action identifiers, including redacted info, redactions, 10/29/14 clarification response, clarification letter, Clarification. Average label length is 29.8 characters.
[AdministrativeUnit] AdministrativeUnitColumn: Administrative unit handling the request, consistently 'Streets And Sanitation' across all records, indicating this log is specific to that department. Contains 1 administrative units, including Streets And Sanitation, with an average identifier length of 22.0 characters.

== Table Description ==
The StrategicSubjectList table contains risk assessment profiles for individuals flagged by Chicago’s criminal justice system under the Strategic Subject List (SSL) initiative. Comprising 10,000 rows and 48 columns, it integrates demographic, behavioral, arrest history, geographic, and administrative attributes to model individual likelihood of involvement in violent or narcotics-related crime. The data includes core demographic traits (age, sex, race), arrest counts categorized by offense type (violent, narcotic, weapons), binary flags for gang affiliation and drug/weapons involvement, and administrative details such as arrest dates and police district assignments. Derived metrics include raw and heat risk scores, residential identifiers (city, state, zip code), census tract and community area codes, and geospatial zones used for spatial risk modeling. Most columns are highly sparse (>90% nulls), reflecting optional, historical, or derived metadata rather than mandatory identifiers. The table is structured for predictive analytics and operational risk prioritization within Chicago’s public safety framework, serving as a foundational dataset for algorithmic risk assessment in law enforcement contexts.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive progress report data for all Chicago Public Schools during the 2011–2012 academic year, integrating demographic, geographic, administrative, and academic performance metrics across 79 columns. It includes foundational school identifiers (e.g., school name, ID, network, address), regulatory status, and safety/environment indicators with standardized icons and scores. Academic performance is detailed through ISAT test results, literacy and math proficiency by grade band (PK–2, 3–5, 6–8), student and teacher attendance rates, misconduct records, parent engagement levels, and special education compliance (IEP). Advanced metrics include algebra performance ratios, Explore/Plan/ACT scores, graduation rates, and college enrollment rates. Geographic attributes map each school to community areas, wards, police districts, and precise latitude/longitude coordinates. Color-coded icons and value-add trends indicate performance levels (Strong/Average/Weak) and academic progress over time, enabling holistic analysis of school accountability, equity, and college readiness across Chicago’s urban landscape.

== Table Description ==
This table contains a comprehensive, school-level progress report for all Chicago Public Schools (CPS) in School Year 2016–2017 (SY1617), integrating foundational metadata, academic performance metrics, climate and culture surveys, NWEA MAP assessment data, operational indicators, demographic comparisons, and post-secondary outcomes. It includes 661 schools with 161 columns spanning: school identification (ID, name, address, contact), fiscal context, recognition awards, and web presence; elementary and middle school academic achievement and growth in reading and math (with percentage metrics and narrative descriptions); NWEA MAP assessment results by grade (2–8) and subject, broken down by performance ratios and student subgroup labels (e.g., 99th percentile) to enable equity analysis; school climate survey data from students, teachers, and families covering environment, instruction, leadership, safety, and more; suspension rates, average lengths, and discipline metrics; attendance, dropout, on-track, and graduation rates compared to CPS-wide averages; and advanced post-secondary outcomes including college enrollment and persistence rates. The dataset also includes geographic coordinates, survey response rates, certification statuses (e.g., Healthy and Creative Schools), and contextual metadata such as URLs and reporting notes. High null rates are present due to sparse data in small schools, grade-level exclusions, or non-reporting categories, reflecting real-world data availability constraints in a large urban district.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive 2012–2013 progress report data for all elementary schools in the Chicago Public Schools system, spanning 460 schools and 83 metrics. It integrates administrative identifiers (school name, ID, network, address), geographic coordinates, and probation status with detailed academic performance and growth metrics in reading and mathematics across grades 3–8. Performance is broken down by student demographic subgroups including race/ethnicity, disability status, English language learner status, and socioeconomic status (free/reduced lunch eligibility). The table also includes school-wide ratings, attendance and suspension rates, school climate classifications, program recognitions (e.g., awards), and operational indicators such as teacher and student attendance. Together, these columns provide a holistic, multi-dimensional evaluation of school performance, equity, and environment for accountability, policy, and research purposes.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive survey data from 7,021 public passenger vehicle chauffeurs in Chicago, capturing their demographic, operational, financial, and experiential profiles across pre-pandemic (2019) and pandemic (2020) periods. It includes foundational identifiers and timestamps, vehicle ownership and insurance details (including wheelchair-accessible vehicle status), employment classification (employee vs. independent contractor), and estimated weekly expenses. Financial metrics encompass net pay, tips, advertising income, hourly earnings, and weekly driving/waiting hours across taxi, livery, and ride-hailing platforms (Uber, Lyft, Via), with insights into platform selection and multi-app usage. The dataset further details chauffeurs' behavioral and experiential responses during the COVID-19 pandemic, including changes in employment status, income sources, access to personal protective equipment (PPE), illness exposure, health and safety concerns, and post-pandemic work intentions. Demographic data includes geographic, educational, and identity-related attributes, supplemented by open-text responses on motivations and challenges. High rates of missing data in later columns reflect the optional or sensitive nature of pandemic-era questions, not data quality issues, making this a rich but nuanced resource for analyzing chauffeur livelihoods under economic and public health stress.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive police sentiment and performance metrics across 7557 geographic units (organized by org_level, city, area, district, and sector), with data aggregated by multiple demographic and socioeconomic dimensions including race, age, sex, education, and income. It captures public sentiment toward police through two primary dimensions: trust (t_) and safety (s_), measured as performance ratios reflecting the proportion of positive responses in each subgroup. The table is divided into three logical batches: (1) foundational geographic and baseline sentiment scores; (2) performance ratios conditioned on the 'listen' behavior — perceived police willingness to listen — with a consistent 45% null rate attributable to incomplete survey responses (not data quality issues); and (3) extended sentiment scores across intersecting demographic groups, including education- and income-specific listening behaviors, with the final two columns ('start_date' and 'end_date') defining the temporal scope of each measurement. All performance ratio columns (t_ and t_listen_ prefixed) share the same 45% null rate, indicating systematic missingness tied to geographic-temporal coverage rather than survey errors. The data enables granular analysis of equitable police perception across intersecting social identities and geographic contexts.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata for 10,000 transportation network segments, capturing both geometric and administrative attributes of street segments in a GIS-enabled transportation database. Each row represents a linear road segment with detailed spatial information including linestring geometry and geometric length, along with connectivity data via node identifiers for network analysis. The dataset includes street names, types, and directional prefixes/suffixes, paired with from/to address ranges on both left and right sides of the street, and logical address boundaries to support address interpolation. Operational attributes indicate one-way travel status, direction of movement, and current status events. Administrative and regulatory context is provided through parity (odd/even) address assignment, FIPS and ZLEV codes for jurisdictional boundaries, and census block identifiers. Zip codes and street cross-reference details enable geocoding and address validation. The table also tracks data lineage with creation and update timestamps, making it suitable for transportation planning, routing systems, emergency response mapping, and urban infrastructure management.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive historical COVID-19 vaccination data aggregated by ZIP code across the United States, spanning from December 2020 to September 2023. It includes daily and cumulative metrics for total vaccine doses administered, first doses, completed vaccine series, and bivalent booster doses, all broken down by age groups. Additionally, the table incorporates population estimates, geographic coordinates (latitude and longitude), and unique record identifiers to enable spatial and demographic analysis. With 10,000 rows and 36 columns, it provides granular, time-series vaccination coverage percentages and trends, supporting public health surveillance, equity assessments, and policy planning at the local level.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata and performance metrics for all Chicago Public Schools (CPS) in School Year 2023–2024 (SY2324), spanning 650 schools and 182 columns. It integrates foundational school information (identifiers, names, locations, contact details, type, and awards), operational and climate data (attendance, suspensions, discipline, survey responses), academic performance indicators (NWEA reading and math growth and attainment for grades 2–8), student outcomes (on-track rates, graduation, college enrollment and persistence), and standardized test results (SAT/PSAT). Additionally, it includes certification statuses for healthy and creative schools, survey-based perceptions of school environment, mobility and truancy rates, geographic coordinates, and contextual links to official reports. While many growth-related metrics (e.g., NWEA, SAT growth) are currently null—likely due to reporting delays or data collection constraints—the table serves as a holistic framework for evaluating school performance, climate, and equity across CPS, with extensive year-over-year comparisons and district-wide benchmarks for context.

== Table Description ==
This table contains a comprehensive spatial street network dataset representing 10,000 road segments with detailed geometric, topological, administrative, and temporal attributes. Each row corresponds to a linestring geometry representing a road segment, linked by from-node (fnode_id) and to-node (tnode_id) identifiers to enable topological network analysis. The dataset includes street names, left/right address ranges (from/to), directional flow indicators (pre_dir, suf_dir, oneway_dir), and administrative classifications (class, tier) that define road hierarchy and operational status. Secondary attributes provide geographic referencing via ZIP and FIPS codes, census block identifiers, cross-street names, and precise geometric lengths. Temporal metadata tracks data lineage through edit/update timestamps, while status flags indicate the current validity or maintenance state of each segment. This table supports advanced spatial analysis, transportation modeling, route planning, and location validation within a geographic information system (GIS) context.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive historical public health data on chlamydia cases among males aged 15–44 in Chicago, spanning the years 2000 to 2014. It includes 79 rows, each representing a unique Chicago community area, and 63 columns that capture annual case counts and incidence rates (with 95% confidence intervals) across 15 years. The data is organized in three chronological batches: 2000–2004 (columns 0–21), 2005–2010 (columns 22–43), and 2010–2014 (columns 44–65). For each year, three metrics are provided: observed chlamydia case counts, point estimate of incidence rate per 100,000 population, and the lower and upper bounds of the 95% confidence interval for that rate. The table provides geographic granularity at the community area level, enabling analysis of regional disparities in chlamydia burden over time. The final three columns (63 total) contain redundant metadata (year, geographic region, demographic category) that are largely null or duplicated across rows, likely artifacts of data structure rather than meaningful variables.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive, week-by-week surveillance data on influenza-related ICU cases in the U.S. across eight influenza seasons (2015-2016 to 2022-2023). It integrates demographic, clinical, and epidemiological metrics, including total ICU case counts, age group distributions (counts and percentages), racial and ethnic breakdowns (counts and proportions), sex distribution, influenza virus subtype prevalence (e.g., A/H1N1, A/H3N2, B), mortality rates, and influenza vaccination reporting. Each row represents a unique reporting week, with columns capturing both raw volumes and normalized percentages to enable trend analysis and disparity identification across population subgroups. The data supports public health monitoring of critical care utilization, equity in healthcare outcomes, and the impact of influenza strains and vaccination efforts on severe disease burden.

== Column Information ==

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive performance and contextual metadata for all Chicago Public Schools (CPS) in School Year 2017–2018 (SY1718), spanning 661 schools and 163 columns. It integrates foundational school identifiers (e.g., name, type, contact), academic achievement metrics (NWEA MAP reading and math growth and attainment by grade 2–8), student growth and attainment ratings, survey-based school climate data (from students, teachers, and families), certification statuses (e.g., Healthy and Creative Schools), discipline indicators (suspensions, misconduct ratios), attendance and truancy rates, dropout and freshman on-track rates, graduation rates (4- and 5-year), standardized test scores (PSAT/SAT), college readiness indicators, and post-secondary enrollment/persistence outcomes. The table also includes CPS-wide benchmarks, performance categories (labeled from 1–99), and administrative metadata (addresses, URLs, fiscal year). High null rates are present in later columns, reflecting sparse reporting for smaller, newer, or specialized schools rather than data quality issues. This dataset serves as a holistic evaluation tool for school effectiveness, student outcomes, and operational health across academic, behavioral, and environmental domains.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive daily and cumulative COVID-19 vaccination data for Chicago residents from December 2020 to September 2023, encompassing over 1,000 rows and 155 columns. It integrates multiple dimensions of vaccination metrics including: total doses administered, first doses, completed vaccine series, booster doses, and bivalent vaccine doses. Each metric is meticulously disaggregated by age group (0–4, 5–11, 12–17, 18–29, 30–39, 40–49, 50–59, 60–69, 70–79, 80+, and unknown), gender, and race/ethnicity (following CDC standards, including Latinx, non-Latinx racial groups, and unknown categories). The data includes both raw counts and population percentages, enabling analysis of vaccination coverage, equity, and temporal trends across demographic segments. Columns are organized into five logical batches: overall vaccination metrics (Batch 1), demographic stratification of first and total doses (Batch 2), granular daily counts by race/ethnicity, age, and gender for first doses and series completion (Batch 3), booster dose administration by demographic (Batch 4), and bivalent vaccine doses by demographic (Batch 5). This table serves as a detailed historical record of Chicago’s public health response, supporting policy analysis, equity assessments, and epidemiological research.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive asthma hospitalization statistics for Chicago from 2000 to 2011, organized by zip code and year. It includes raw hospitalization counts, crude rates (per population), and age-adjusted rates (for ages 5–64), each accompanied by lower and upper 95% confidence intervals. The data spans 49 geographic units (primarily zip codes) across 12 years, divided into three chronological batches: 2000–2004 (columns 0–29), 2004–2008 (columns 30–58), and 2008–2011 (columns 59–87). Age-adjustment enables consistent year-to-year comparisons by accounting for demographic shifts. Metadata columns (Region, Year, Category) are present but sparse, as they redundantly describe the same geographic scope (Chicago) and demographic focus (ages 5–64) across all rows. The table serves as a longitudinal public health resource for tracking asthma burden, disparities, and trends over time in Chicago's neighborhoods.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive records of building permits issued in Illinois, primarily in Chicago and surrounding areas, spanning 10,000 rows and 116 columns. It captures the full lifecycle of construction projects, including core permit metadata (status, type, application/issuance dates), operational processing metrics, and detailed work descriptions. The table documents up to 15 contact roles associated with each permit—primarily contractors, architects, and property owners—each with name, city, state, and zip code; however, contact fields beyond the first few are largely null due to sparse participation. Financial data includes fee payments, waivers, and total reported project costs. Geographic and administrative identifiers are provided via PIN (Property Index Number), PIN9, community area, census tract, and ward, enabling granular spatial analysis within Chicago. Precise geographic coordinates (latitude, longitude, x/y coordinates, and location geometry) are included for each permit, allowing for mapping and spatial querying. The dataset serves as a foundational resource for municipal planning, regulatory compliance, economic impact analysis, and construction activity monitoring.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata and performance metrics for all Chicago Public Elementary Schools during the 2013–2014 school year. It integrates foundational institutional information (school ID, address, contact details, website, probation status, and certifications), school climate and operational indicators derived from student and teacher surveys (supportive environment, teacher collaboration, family involvement), NWEA reading and math performance data (including both growth and attainment percentiles across grade levels and school-wide aggregates), discipline metrics (suspension and misconduct rates), attendance rates for students and teachers, geographic coordinates and spatial geometry for mapping, and institutional classification data (e.g., school type, organization, year). The dataset enables multi-dimensional analysis of school performance, combining academic achievement, behavioral climate, operational efficiency, and geographic context, with comparative data spanning 2012–2013 for trend analysis.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata on park infrastructure and amenities across Chicago (deprecated as of November 2016), encompassing 581 park locations with 75 attributes. It combines foundational geographic and administrative data (park names, addresses, zip codes, ward assignments, acreage, and park classification) with detailed binary indicators and volume counts for 50+ recreational, cultural, and sports facilities. Features include common amenities such as playgrounds, gymnasiums, tennis courts, spray features, and basketball courts, as well as less common ones like golf courses, pools, nature centers, boat launches, and community gardens. Some columns aggregate counts (e.g., baseball/softball/t-ball fields), while others capture administrative status and address details. The dataset enables spatial, functional, and comparative analysis of park accessibility and facility distribution, though many amenities are sparsely represented. Designed for urban planning and public service evaluation, this deprecated dataset remains valuable for historical analysis of Chicago’s park system.
