== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata for cases in the Micro-Market Recovery Program, spanning 10,000 records and 37 columns. It integrates core administrative lifecycle data with detailed geospatial and administrative routing information, primarily focused on Chicago’s municipal framework. The first 19 columns capture case creation timestamps, standardized classification codes, status indicators, resolution outcomes, and hearing events—reflecting the progression of cases through regulatory processes, with expected nulls in resolution and hearing fields due to ongoing or inactive cases. The remaining 18 columns provide precise location attributes (street address, latitude/longitude, ZIP code, community area, ward), administrative assignments (court room, zone), and record identifiers, enabling spatial analysis and efficient case routing. High null rates in optional fields such as central business district and post direction reflect non-mandatory data collection. Together, this table supports end-to-end case management, geographic targeting, and operational analytics for municipal recovery initiatives.

== Column Information ==
[Timestamp] add date/time: Timestamp marking when the case was initially recorded; examples: 2005-04-11T08:15, 2020-12-03T14:11, 2024-06-17T12:20 Timestamps ranging from 2005-04-11T08:15 to 2024-06-17T12:20 across 10,000 records

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata for alternative fuel station locations across Illinois, with a primary focus on electric vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure and secondary support for E85, CNG, LPG, and biodiesel (BD) fueling stations. It combines 38 columns that capture identifying information, physical addresses, operational status, access rules, payment methods, and fuel-specific attributes. The dataset includes detailed counts and specifications for Level 2 and DC fast chargers, network affiliations (with web links), precise geographic coordinates (latitude/longitude), confirmation and last update timestamps, ownership details, and federal agency associations. Additional fields document fuel availability (e.g., E85 blend ratios, CNG pressure levels), access hours, future deployment dates, and conditional attributes with high null rates reflecting optional or site-specific configurations. The table supports spatial analysis, infrastructure planning, accessibility assessment, and operational management of alternative fueling networks for both public and private stakeholders.

== Column Information ==

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata linking inspection events and regulatory violations within Chicago's Micro-Market Recovery Program. It combines administrative, spatial, and enforcement data across 43 columns, capturing details such as permit/case types, inspection dates and statuses, inspector assignments, violation codes and descriptions, compliance comments, and geolocated address information (including street-level addresses, zone, ward, and community area). Each row represents a violation-inspection record tied to a specific physical location, enabling end-to-end tracking of code enforcement actions from inspection initiation through violation documentation and spatial analysis. While many fields contain sparse or null values—reflecting optional annotations and structured reporting conventions—the dataset supports audit trails, regulatory compliance monitoring, and neighborhood-level trend analysis of code violations across Chicago.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive residential lending application and denial data spanning 2020–2023, aligned with U.S. CFPB HMDA reporting standards. It includes 46 columns divided into two core segments: (1) loan and institutional characteristics (columns 0–22), such as reporting year, lending institution, census tract location, loan type, purpose, amount, interest rate, term, fees, lien status, property type, and denial reasons; and (2) applicant and co-applicant demographic information (columns 23–45), including detailed race, ethnicity, and sex categories—each allowing multiple selections to reflect self-reported identities. The dataset captures both originated loans and denials, with notable nulls in optional or conditionally reported fields such as specific fees or denial codes. Designed for regulatory compliance and fair lending analysis, this table enables granular analysis of mortgage access, disparities, and lending patterns across geographic and demographic dimensions.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive transportation network segment data for 10,000 road segments, structured for GIS, routing, and network analysis applications. It combines administrative, geometric, address, and metadata attributes across 50 columns. Core components include origin/destination node identifiers (fnode_id, tnode_id), linear spatial geometries (the_geom), street naming components (pre_dir, street_nam, street_typ, suf_dir), left/right address ranges (l_f_add, l_t_add, r_f_add, r_t_add), and logical address boundaries (logiclf, logiclt). The dataset captures facility classification, status flags, one-way traffic direction, travel direction indicators, and geometric length measurements. Administrative context is provided via FIPS codes, ZIP codes, census block IDs, and zoning levels (zlev). Temporal metadata includes creation and update timestamps, along with system-generated edit and usage flags (edit_type, create_use, update_use), many of which exhibit high null rates, indicating optional or system-derived annotations. Binary flags for parity and direction support address validation and geocoding workflows. The table is designed to support transportation network validation, address geocoding, routing algorithms, and data lineage tracking in municipal or regional transportation systems.

Each record links a lobbyist to a client and documents total spending over defined reporting periods, with timestamps indicating record creation.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive public health statistics on gonorrhea cases among males aged 15–44 in Chicago, spanning the years 2000 to 2014. It includes 79 rows representing community areas (or geographic units) and 63 columns organized chronologically into three batches: 2000–2004 (columns 0–21), 2005–2010 (columns 22–43), and 2010–2014 (columns 44–65). For each year, the data provides three key metrics: observed case counts, estimated incidence rates per 100,000 population, and the lower and upper bounds of the 95% confidence interval for those rates. The table enables spatial-temporal analysis of gonorrhea burden across Chicago’s community areas, with additional contextual metadata (e.g., data reliability notes, geographic/demographic tags) included in the 2010–2014 batch, though these are largely sparse due to consistent reporting patterns. The dataset supports epidemiological research, public health planning, and intervention evaluation by offering granular, year-by-year incidence trends with statistical uncertainty estimates.

== Table Description ==
Tracks weekly performance metrics for time to issue easy permits in buildings, comparing actual average days against a target benchmark over a 3-year period.

== Column Information ==
[Timestamp] week: Weekly timestamps spanning from May 30, 2011, to July 28, 2014, with samples including 2011-05-30, 2012-12-25, and 2014-07-28. Timestamps ranging from 2011-05-30T00:00 to 2014-07-28T00:00 across 166 records

[PerformanceRatio] target: Target days to issue permits, consistently set between 1.00 and 1.30, with common values of 1.25, 1.20, and 1.30. Performance ratios ranging from 1 to 1.30 with an average of 1.25 across 166 records.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive historical gonorrhea case data for females aged 15–44 in Chicago, spanning the years 2000 to 2014. It includes 79 rows representing community areas and 63 columns organized chronologically by year, with each year represented by four metrics: observed case count, point estimate of incidence rate per 100,000 population, and the lower and upper bounds of the 95% confidence interval for that rate. The data is structured in three chronological batches: 2000–2004 (columns 0–21), 2005–2010 (columns 22–43), and 2010–2014 (columns 44–65), with 2010 overlapping between batches. All data is aggregated at the community area level, capturing geographic granularity across Chicago. Missing values indicate incomplete reporting or insufficient population denominators for reliable estimation. The table serves as a public health surveillance resource for tracking trends in gonorrhea incidence among young adult females over a 15-year period.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive budget allocation and public sentiment data from the 2021 Fiscal Year Budget Survey, encompassing 10,000 survey records across 56 columns. It captures detailed financial assignments (in $1,000 units), citizen-perceived performance rankings (on a 1–10 scale), and binary indicators for additional resource requests or reallocations across nine key municipal departments—including police, public health, infrastructure, and community programs. The first half of the columns (0–28) focus on baseline budget allocations and performance evaluations, while the second half (29–56) detail proposed funding sources (e.g., tax increases), reallocation priorities, and whether departments were net recipients or providers of additional funds. The table also includes a counter for record tracking, a year field for temporal context, and one open-ended text column capturing qualitative feedback. This dataset enables analysis of the relationship between public perception, funding decisions, and fiscal policy proposals in municipal governance.

== Table Description ==
A dataset capturing business owner information including account identifiers, legal entity names, individual owner details (first, middle, last names, suffix), and associated administrative roles, with a significant portion of middle initial and legal entity owner fields being null due to optional reporting.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive historical daily records of COVID-19 cases, deaths, and hospitalizations in the United States from March 2020 to May 2024, with detailed demographic stratifications. It includes 1540 rows (one per day) and 58 columns, organized into two logical groups: (1) daily aggregated totals and demographic breakdowns for cases and deaths by age group (0-17, 18-29, 30-39, 40-49, 50-59, 60-69, 70-79, 80+), gender (female, male, unknown), and race/ethnicity (Latino, Asian, Black, White, Other, Unknown); and (2) additional demographic breakdowns specifically for deaths and hospitalizations, with some columns potentially overlapping or extending the first batch. A null 'Year' column (likely a metadata artifact) is present but contains no valid data. The dataset enables granular public health analysis, including temporal trends and disparities across demographic groups in infection, mortality, and hospitalization rates.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata for transportation permits issued by the Department of Transportation, encompassing the full lifecycle of permits related to roadwork, vehicle authorizations, and right-of-way activities. It combines administrative and operational details (columns 0–23) such as application submission and processing dates, permit issuance and expiration, entity and contact information, work types, fees, and status tracking, with detailed geospatial and inspection metadata (columns 24–46) including inspection types, dates, results, street address ranges, directional and suffix information, facility placement and closure indicators, geographic coordinates (X/Y, latitude/longitude), and spatial geometry. While many geospatial and inspection fields are sparse due to being context-specific or optional, they are essential for geospatial analysis, compliance verification, and detailed permit documentation. The table supports end-to-end permit management, regulatory compliance, operational reporting, and spatial planning for transportation infrastructure activities across 10,000 permit records.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata for 10,000 buildings in Chicago, integrating foundational structural attributes, geospatial characteristics, administrative zoning, and data provenance information across 43 columns. It includes unique building identifiers (bldg_id), spatial geometry (the_geom), street addresses (f_add1, t_add1, pre_dir1, st_name1, st_type1), building status (bldg_statu), construction and activation timestamps (bldg_creat, bldg_activ), story count (stories), and administrative use codes (create_use). Additional columns capture building units, year built, square footage, condition, and geometric measurements. Crucially, the table also tracks data editing history and quality control metadata (e.g., creation/modification timestamps, data sources, validation flags), reflecting its role as a curated urban asset registry. While many fields such as building names (bldg_name1), unit names, and comments are sparse, this reflects optional or legacy reporting rather than data gaps. The dataset serves as a foundational geospatial inventory for urban planning, property management, and infrastructure analysis, with high emphasis on spatial accuracy and data lineage.

== Table Description ==
This table contains a comprehensive longitudinal performance and operational profile of Chicago Public High Schools for the 2013–2014 academic year, integrating demographic, academic, operational, and geographic metrics. It includes foundational school attributes such as location, contact information, administrative structure, CPS policy classification, and probation status, alongside school climate indicators derived from stakeholder surveys. Academic performance is captured through multiple standardized assessments—including NWEA (reading and math growth/attainment percentiles for grades 3–11), EXPLORE, PLAN, and ACT—providing both attainment (current performance relative to national norms) and growth (year-over-year progress) metrics. Additional key indicators include on-track rates, graduation rates, college readiness metrics, student and teacher attendance, disciplinary incidents, and spatial data. While the dataset spans 97 columns and 188 schools, many columns exhibit high null rates due to incomplete reporting across grade levels or testing cycles, reflecting structural data collection limitations rather than data quality issues. The table serves as a holistic institutional snapshot for evaluating school performance, equity, and operational health within the Chicago Public Schools system during the 2013–2014 cycle.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive metadata for all Chicago Public Schools in School Year 2021, encompassing administrative, geographic, demographic, operational, and performance dimensions. It includes foundational identifiers (school ID, name, type), physical and digital contact details (addresses, websites, social media), leadership and staff contacts (up to seven roles), and geographic coordinates. The table captures educational structure through grade levels offered, enrollment data segmented by race, income, and special populations (e.g., ELL, special education, refugee students), and operational indicators such as preschool/kindergarten availability, school hours, and transportation services. It also includes binary flags for specialized programs (bilingual, inclusive preschool, etc.), academic performance metrics (graduation and college enrollment rates), administrative network affiliations, and enrollment status dates. While some columns exhibit sparsity due to optional or eligibility-based reporting, the dataset as a whole enables detailed equity analysis, resource allocation planning, and institutional profiling across the district.

== Table Description ==
This table contains community-level general fertility rates for Chicago from 1999 to 2009, with each row representing one of Chicago's community areas. It includes annual point estimates of births per 1,000 women aged 15–44, along with corresponding 95% confidence intervals (lower and upper bounds), spanning two time periods: 1999–2004 (columns 0–18) and 2004–2009 (columns 19–37). The dataset captures geographic and temporal trends in reproductive behavior across Chicago’s neighborhoods, with additional metadata columns for Region, Year, and Status in the later batch. All fertility rates are standardized to the same demographic denominator (women aged 15–44) and expressed as performance ratios, enabling longitudinal and cross-community comparisons of fertility patterns over a decade.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive public health statistics on births to mothers aged 15–19 years across Chicago's community areas, spanning the years 1999 to 2009. For each year (1999–2004 in columns 0–24 and 2004–2009 in columns 25–48), the data includes the raw count of teen births, the birth rate per 1,000 females aged 15–19, and the lower and upper bounds of the 95% confidence interval for that rate, enabling analysis of both geographic variation by community area and temporal trends over time. The final three columns (Year, Region, Category) serve as static metadata identifiers, consistently labeling the data as pertaining to Chicago adolescent births, though they contain mostly null values due to their uniformity across the dataset. With 78 rows representing community areas and 46 columns capturing six years of dual metrics (count and rate with confidence intervals), this dataset supports epidemiological research, policy evaluation, and public health interventions targeting adolescent fertility in Chicago.

== Table Description ==
This table contains a comprehensive profile of all Chicago Public Schools for the school year 2018–2019 (SY1819), encompassing foundational metadata, student demographics, operational details, support services, performance metrics, and administrative information. It includes institutional identifiers, school type, contact details (address, phone, website), enrollment statistics, and geographic coordinates. Student demographic data covers racial/ethnic composition, English learner status, special education enrollment, and Title I eligibility. Operational details include school hours, transportation services, and language programs. Performance metrics encompass graduation rates, college enrollment rates, and ACT scores, alongside school ratings and status classifications (e.g., Tier 1–4, Priority School). Administrative contacts for up to seven key roles are included, along with network affiliations, enrollment status flags, and key operational dates. While many fields are standardized, some contact and performance fields are sparse due to structural variations across schools. This dataset serves as a central reference for school identification, demographic analysis, performance benchmarking, and policy planning within Chicago Public Schools.

Each row represents a unique road segment with start/end coordinates, intersecting streets, and real-time speed data, useful for traffic monitoring and urban planning.

== Column Information ==
[RecordIdentifier] segmentid: Unique identifier for each traffic segment, such as '604', '989', '1169'. Contains 1,257 unique record identifiers, including examples such as "604", "989", "1169", "394", "1029".
[EntityName] street: Primary street name of the segment, including 'Cicero', 'Harlem', 'Lake Shore Dr'. 71 unique entities identified, including "Cicero", "Harlem", "Lake Shore Dr", "Ashland", "Pulaski", with an average name length of 7.5 characters.
[IncidentType] direction: Direction of travel along the segment, such as 'SB' (southbound), 'NB' (northbound), 'EB' (eastbound), 'WB' (westbound). 8 incident types, including SB, NB, WB, EB, SE
[Address] from_street: Starting cross street or intersection, e.g., '76th', 'Western', '59th'. Contains 168 unique addresses or address components, including examples such as "76th", "Western", "59th", "Harlem", "Pulaski". The average length of the address strings is 7.0 characters.
[Address] to_street: Ending cross street or intersection, e.g., '103rd', 'Central Park', 'Mendota'. Contains 168 unique addresses or address components, including examples such as "103rd", "Central Park", "Oak Park", "Mendota", "Chicago". The average length of the address strings is 7.0 characters.
[ShapeMeasurement] length: Length of the segment in miles, ranging from 0.07 to 1.94, with a mean of 0.55. Geometric measurements ranging from 0.07 to 1.94 (mean: 0.55, median: 0.50)
[IncidentType]  street_heading: Cardinal direction of the street orientation, such as 'S' (south), 'W' (west), 'N' (north), 'E' (east). 4 incident types, including S, W, N, E
[DescriptionText]  comments: Additional notes on segment conditions, e.g., 'Outside City Limits', 'Oneway', 'IDOT Signals Possible'. Text descriptions with an average length of 19.7 characters. Key terms include: N/A. Example entries: "Outside City Limits", "Outside City Limits", "Oneway", "IDOT Signals Possible", "Western Blvd Run Parallel on the East Side".
[GeographicCoordinate] start_longitude: Longitude of the segment's starting point, ranging from -87.90 to -87.52, mean -87.69. Geographic coordinates ranging from -87.90 to -87.52 with a mean value of -87.69
[GeographicCoordinate]  start_latitude: Latitude of the segment's starting point, ranging from 41.66 to 42.01, mean 41.85. Geographic coordinates ranging from 41.66 to 42.01 with a mean value of 41.85
[GeographicCoordinate] end_longitude: Longitude of the segment's ending point, ranging from -87.90 to -87.52, mean -87.69. Geographic coordinates ranging from -87.90 to -87.52 with a mean value of -87.69

[PerformanceMetric]  current_speed: Current average speed on the segment in mph, ranging from -1 (invalid) to 55, mean 14.77. Performance metrics ranging from -1 to 55 with an average of 14.77 across 1,257 records.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive chlamydia case statistics for females aged 15–44 in Chicago, spanning the years 2000 to 2014. It includes annual counts of observed cases, point estimates of incidence rates per 100,000 population, and corresponding 95% confidence intervals (lower and upper bounds) for each community area. The data is organized across 63 columns, grouped by five-year intervals: 2000–2004 (columns 0–21), 2005–2010 (columns 22–43), and 2010–2014 (columns 44–65). Each year within these ranges is represented by three columns: total cases, incidence rate, and confidence interval bounds. The first columns include geographic identifiers and community area names, enabling spatial analysis. In the later batch (2010–2014), additional sparse metadata columns indicate year, geographic region, and demographic category, reinforcing the scope of the data. The dataset is aggregated at the community area level, providing granular, time-series public health surveillance data for tracking chlamydia trends among young women in Chicago over a 15-year period.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive energy usage and building characteristics data for 10,000 buildings across 77 community areas in 2010. It integrates demographic, structural, and energy consumption metrics across three key dimensions: (1) foundational building attributes including geographic identifiers (community area, census block), building type/subtype, population, units, age, and occupancy rates; (2) detailed monthly and annual electricity (kWh) and natural gas (therms) consumption, including total usage, per-square-foot normalization, and statistical summaries (mean, quartiles, standard deviation, min, max); and (3) financial and performance indicators derived from energy usage patterns. The data enables analysis of energy efficiency trends, spatial disparities in consumption, and correlations between building characteristics (e.g., size, age, occupancy) and energy demand, supporting urban planning, sustainability initiatives, and utility demand forecasting.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive prenatal care statistics for Chicago community areas spanning the years 1999 to 2009, organized across 48 columns. The first 26 columns (1999–2004) capture annual birth volumes, percentages of mothers initiating prenatal care in the first trimester, and associated confidence intervals, aggregated by community area. The subsequent 22 columns (2004–2009) extend these metrics with similar performance indicators, including additional metadata such as year, region, and category (largely constant or sparse), along with a 'warning' column that flags data reliability concerns for years with low birth counts. The dataset enables longitudinal analysis of maternal health access and equity across Chicago’s community areas, highlighting trends, disparities, and data quality issues over an 11-year period.

== Table Description ==
This table contains comprehensive profile metadata for all Chicago Public Schools during the 2016–2017 school year (SY1617), encompassing administrative, demographic, operational, geographic, and performance-related attributes. It includes foundational school identifiers, contact details, leadership information, geographic location, website presence, and grade levels offered. It further details student demographics by race/ethnicity and subgroups (e.g., low-income, special education, English learners), school schedule and facility accessibility, and eligibility for federal and state programs such as Title I and bilingual education. Supplementary data includes GPS coordinates, academic performance metrics (ACT scores, graduation rates, college enrollment), school ratings, contact information for up to seven staff members, physical addresses, transportation services, and temporal context. While many fields are critical for equity analysis, resource allocation, and school accountability, a significant number of columns exhibit high null rates due to inconsistent or optional reporting across schools, reflecting the complexity and variability of data collection in a large urban district.

== Table Description ==
A comprehensive dataset tracking asbestos-related demolition and renovation notifications in Chicago, including spatial, temporal, and administrative details for over 10,000 projects from 1992 to 2024, with geolocated building sites and applicant information.

== Column Information ==
[IncidentType] notification type: Two types of notifications: NOI DEMO/RENOVATION and NOI NESHAP 2 incident types, including NOI DEMO/RENOVATION, NOI NESHAP
[Address] address: Physical addresses of demolition/renovation sites, e.g., "1908 S DRAKE AVE", "1328 W CARMEN AVE", "1821 N MOHAWK ST" Contains 7,244 unique addresses or address components, including examples such as "1908 S DRAKE AVE", "1328 W CARMEN AVE", "1821 N MOHAWK ST", "1923 W OAKDALE AVE", "1511 W IRVING PARK RD". The average length of the address strings is 17.2 characters.
[StreetDetail] street number from: Starting street number of the project site, e.g., "1660", "10603", "1412" Street details including 1,974 unique components such as "1660", "10603", "1412", "1850", "1451", with an average length of 4.1 characters.
[StreetDetail] street number to: Ending street number for range-based addresses, e.g., "1709", "1416", "1255" Street details including 411 unique components such as "1709", "1416", "1255", "1610", "11503", with an average length of 4.0 characters.
[StreetDetail] direction: Cardinal direction of the street, e.g., "W", "S" Street details including 4 unique components such as "W", "W", "S", "W", "S", with an average length of 1.0 characters.
[StreetDetail] street name: Name of the street, e.g., "SUPERIOR", "87TH", "LA SALLE" Street details including 583 unique components such as "SUPERIOR", "87TH", "LA SALLE", "CLYBOURN", "LONGWOOD", with an average length of 6.6 characters.
[StreetDetail] street type: Type of street, e.g., "ST", "AVE", "RD" Street details including 11 unique components such as "ST", "ST", "AVE", "ST", "RD", with an average length of 2.5 characters.
[BusinessEntity] owner/applicant: Owner or applicant entity, e.g., "CITY OF CHICAGO", "MCDONAGH DEMOLITION INC*", "MARCIN WOJTOWICZ" 3,873 business entities identified, including CITY OF CHICAGO, SMOLKA, CITY OF CHICAGO, DEPARTMENT OF BUILDINGS, MCDONAGH DEMOLITION INC*, MARCIN WOJTOWICZ. Average name length is 18.3 characters.
[EventDate] start date: Date the demolition or renovation activity began, e.g., 1992-10-31, 2020-05-15, 2024-01-10 Event dates ranging from 1992-10-31T00:00 to 2024-06-18T00:00 across 10,000 records
[EventDate] end date: Date the demolition or renovation activity ended, e.g., 1993-02-03, 2023-12-01, 2219-05-03 Event dates ranging from 1993-02-03T00:00 to 2219-05-03T00:00 across 6,219 records
